en.extract:
The long-billed plover (Thinornis placidus) is a species of wading bird in the family Charadriidae. It can be found in Bangladesh, Bhutan, Brunei, Cambodia, China, Hong Kong, India, Indonesia, Japan, Laos, Malaysia, Mongolia, Myanmar, Nepal, North Korea, Russia, South Korea, Sri Lanka, Taiwan, Thailand, and Vietnam. The long-billed plover is a migratory bird, so it breeds and spends the winter in different parts of its range. This bird can often be spotted along the shores of rivers, streams, in wetlands, and rice fields. It forages on the shoreline primarily for aquatic insects, insect larvae, and other invertebrates. It is difficult to distinguish between male and female individuals because of their similar plumage. The breeding season starts at the end of February or early March and ends in July. A male and a female forms a monogamous pair and maintains their territory throughout the breeding season. A global population survey in 2016 assessed the long-billed plover as a species of least concern on the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) Red List.


Taxonomy
The long-billed plover is one of seven species of plovers placed in the genus Thinornis of the family Charadriidae that includes plovers, lapwings, and dotterels. Charadriidae is one of the 17 families under the order Charadriiformes that comprises a wide variety of shorebirds, such as gulls, terns, auks, puffins, sandpipers, lapwings, plovers, and allies. The long-billed plover was first described by J. E. Gray and G. R. Gray in 1863, and no subspecies are recognised.


Description


Measurement and plumage

The long-billed plover is a medium-sized wading bird about 19–20 centimetres (7.5–7.9 inches), and weighing around 41–70 grams (1.4–2.5 oz). The different body parts of males and females are similar in size. On average, the wing length is 14–15 centimetres (5.5–5.9 in), its tail length 7–8 centimetres (2.8–3.1 in), and the bill is about 2 centimetres (0.79 in) long. Both males and females have a grayish brown back and a white belly and throat. The forehead is white with a black band that runs from one eye to the other. The white patch of the forehead extends to the base of the bill. There is a black breast band below the throat. The eyes have a yellow ring around them, and a white stripe above. Even though the male and female have a similar appearance, their breeding plumage (summer plumage) has a few differences that can help distinguish between the two sexes. The black band on the forehead and on the breast of the male is wider and more distinct than that of the female. The feathers around the eyes of an adult breeding male are much darker than those of a female. Furthermore, the male's white eyestripe extends more distinctly behind the eye compared to that of the female. Juveniles look quite similar to adult individuals, however they lack the dark forehead band of adult birds. The breast band of juveniles is narrower and less distinct than that of adult males.


Confusion with other similar species

The long-billed plover is often confused with other species of plover such as the little-ringed plover, because of range overlap and similarity in appearance. However, the two species can still be distinguished from each other. The long-billed plover is larger than the little-ringed plover. As the name suggests, the long-billed plover has a longer and thicker bill than the little-ringed plover. Adult breeding male individuals of little-ringed plover have a solid black bar above the base of the bill, in addition to the black forehead band. The feathers around the eyes of little-ringed plover are black unlike the brown cheek feathers of long-billed plover. Furthermore, the yellow eye-ring of the little-ringed plover is much more distinct than that of long-billed plover. The breast band of the little-ringed plover is usually wider at the center than on the sides, whereas the breast band of long-billed plover has more or less uniform thickness.


Distribution and habitat
The long-billed plover is widely distributed throughout South and East Asia. It is quite uncommon throughout its range and occurs in small numbers. Breeding ground generally includes western, northern, and central parts of China, Japan, North Korea, and South Korea. Many instances of breeding activity have been reported in the Bureya river and other parts of far east Russia, Honshu island in Japan, southeastern China, and in the western Arunachal Pradesh in India near the Himalayas. This species has also been spotted during its breeding season in Singapore. The long-billed plover travels south of its breeding range to spend the winter. It generally winters throughout eastern Nepal, northeast India to Indochina, southern China, South Korea, Honshu, Shikoku, and Kiyushu islands of Japan, Taiwan, Myanmar, Malaysia, and Thailand. Populations on the three main islands of Japan do not generally migrate south, and are year-round residents. Only the population in Hokkaido travel south to winter in warmer climates.

Long-billed plovers prefer to inhabit the shores of rivers, streams, and lakes with small, round pebbles and rocks. They tend to nest on small temporary shingle islands or pebble spits that form between the different branches of meandering mountain rivers. These islands cannot be prone to frequent flooding, and need to be at least 600 square metres (6,500 ft2) in area. The islands also need to have large patches of stones and pebbles that are 30–60 millimetres (1.2–2.4 in) in size. Their habitats are often surrounded by shrubs, willows, and young forests. Long-billed plovers can also be found in freshwater wetland habitats and rice fields in the winter. These birds tend to avoid sandy beaches, mudflats, and areas with large boulders.


Behavior


Vocalization
The long-billed plover has a wide variety of calls for different situations. They tend to get highly vocal during the breeding season. They can be seen flapping their wings rapidly, flying over their territories, and emitting a "fi-fi-fi-fi-fi" call. Males sometimes make a quiet "gee gee" call when they expand their chest to intimidate other rival males. When they are vigilant, they make a single rising "pyuoo-" call. When they are sheltering themselves from a predator or any threat, they emit a call like, "pip-pip-pip".


Food and feeding
Very little information is available on the feeding habit of the long-billed plover. Its diet is thought to primarily include aquatic insects and other invertebrates. These birds have been observed to prey on midges, dragonflies, larvae of beetles, flies, and earthworms. Long-billed plovers forage along the shoreline and when they spot a prey, they move towards it quickly and capture it with their long bill. They do not seem to consume any plant material.
Similar species in the genus Charadrius such as the Common ringed plover and the Kentish plover have been observed to prey on mysid shrimps. The diet of long-billed plovers may also consist of similar crustaceans.


Breeding
Long-billed plovers exhibit a monogamous breeding system in which a male and a female form a pair and establish a territory that they maintain. Females, however, can abandon their nests sometimes to breed with other males. Males generally do not leave their territories. Both males and females take part in territorial disputes. They fly around their territory and let out loud calls. They puff their chest, and often jump, and peck to ward off any unwelcoming intruder. Males begin their breeding activity towards the end of February or in early March. The male displaces gravel with its feet and makes a shallow pit or depression, about 4 centimetres (1.6 in) deep and 13 centimetres (5.1 in) in diameter, by pressing its chest against the ground. The male attracts a female by calling her. When a female comes to the nest, the male spreads his tail for courtship display. The female passes under the tail of the male and enters the nest. The male makes many of these pits on the ground and guides the female from one pit to another, and the female eventually lays eggs in one of nests. The female lays one egg every 48 hours until she has a clutch of four eggs. The diameter and length of an egg is typically around 3.5 and 2.6 centimetres (1.4 and 1.0 in) respectively. The eggs generally weigh around 10–13 grams (0.35–0.46 oz). The eggs are pear-shaped, and they are grayish brown or bluish gray in color with tiny brown spots. Both male and female take part in nest maintenance. They line their nest with small pebbles, twigs, small pieces of bark, and other dry plant parts. Both male and female also carry out incubation of eggs. Eggs normally hatch 29 days after the start of incubation. Incubation period can vary because of human disturbance. Long-billed plovers show a decent amount of parental care. If a predator approaches a nest, the parents try to intimidate the enemy and chase it away by making loud distress calls. Furthermore, on very hot summer days, the parents protect the eggs from overheating by standing over them and shading them from the sun. Long-billed plovers give birth to precocial offspring, meaning that the hatchlings can move around and feed by themselves shortly after hatching. The parents constantly monitor the hatchlings and are always on the lookout for any signs of danger. The hatchlings gain body mass at the rate of 1 gram (0.035 oz) per day, and they become ready to fly when they are 40 days old. Fledglings remain together with their parents for a short time before they leave the nest and migrate to their wintering grounds.
Long-billed plovers tend to return to the same breeding for several consecutive years. This indicates that the same pairs occupy the same nesting sites every year. The pairs tend to be attracted to the same breeding sites year after year. They begin to establish territories when the area still remains covered with snow, suggesting that they must have previous knowledge of the suitability of the site.


Conservation status
The long-billed plover is listed as a species of least concern on the IUCN Red List because of its wide distribution and large home range. It has a distribution range that exceeds 20,000 square kilometres (7,700 mi2). The global population seems to be decreasing, but the rate of decline is not drastic enough to make this species vulnerable. Population decline has to be more than 30% over ten years or three generations for a species to be deemed vulnerable. Even though long-billed plovers are not vulnerable or threatened now, their population might decline rapidly in the future due to the loss of suitable breeding grounds. Conservation efforts should include protection and restoration of breeding habitats. Habitat restoration projects can provide new breeding habitats through the artificial construction of gravel banks and pebble islands.


References


External links

Long-billed plover
Little-ringed plover
Long-billed plover habitat

The '''long-billed plover''' ('''''Thinornis placidus''''') is a species of [[Wader|wading bird]] in the family [[Charadriidae]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=8294655EB86B37C1|title=Charadrius placidus (Long-billed Plover) - Avibase|website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org|access-date=2019-10-12}}</ref> It can be found in [[Bangladesh]], [[Bhutan]], [[Brunei]], [[Cambodia]], [[China]], [[Hong Kong]], [[India]], [[Indonesia]], [[Japan]], [[Laos]], [[Malaysia]], [[Mongolia]], [[Myanmar]], [[Nepal]], [[North Korea]], [[Russia]], [[South Korea]], [[Sri Lanka]], [[Taiwan]], [[Thailand]], and [[Vietnam]].<ref name=":0" /> The long-billed plover is a [[Bird migration|migratory bird]], so it breeds and spends the winter in different parts of its range.<ref name=":1" /> This bird can often be spotted along the shores of rivers, streams, in [[wetland]]s, and rice fields.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Lethaby|first=Nick|date=December 2006|title=The Identification of Long-billed Plover|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/281628327|journal=BirdingAsia|pages=48–54|access-date=2019-10-12|via=ResearchGate}}</ref> It forages on the shoreline primarily for aquatic insects, insect [[larva]]e, and other [[invertebrate]]s.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last=Uchida|first=Hiroshi|date=2007|title=Long-billed Plover|url=http://www.bird-research.jp/1_shiryo/seitai/ikaruchi.pdf|journal=Bird Research News|volume=4|pages=1–3|access-date=2019-10-12}}</ref> It is difficult to distinguish between male and female individuals because of their similar [[plumage]].<ref name=":2" /> The breeding season starts at the end of February or early March and ends in July.<ref name=":2" /> A male and a female forms a [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous pair]] and maintains their territory throughout the breeding season.<ref name=":2" /> A global population survey in 2016 assessed the long-billed plover as a species of least concern on the [[IUCN Red List|International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) Red List]].<ref>{{Cite journal|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|title=The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|doi=10.2305/iucn.uk.2016-3.rlts.t22693767a93421626.en|access-date=2019-10-13|year=2016|doi-access=free}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
The long-billed plover is one of seven species of [[plover]]s placed in the genus ''[[Thinornis]]'' of the family [[Charadriidae]] that includes [[plover]]s, [[lapwing]]s, and [[Eurasian dotterel|dotterels]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://nhpbs.org/wild/charadriidae.asp|title=Charadriidae - plovers, lapwings, dotterals, killdeers {{!}} Wildlife Journal Junior|website=nhpbs.org|access-date=2019-10-13}}</ref> [[Charadriidae]] is one of the 17 families under the order [[Charadriiformes]] that comprises a wide variety of shorebirds, such as [[gull]]s, [[tern]]s, [[auk]]s, [[puffin]]s, [[sandpiper]]s, [[lapwing]]s, [[plover]]s, and allies.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britannica.com/animal/charadriiform|title=Charadriiform {{!}} bird order|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en|access-date=2019-10-13}}</ref> The long-billed plover was first described by J. E. Gray and G. R. Gray in 1863, and no subspecies are recognised.<ref>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=7 November 2025 }}</ref>

== Description ==
=== Measurement and plumage ===
[[File:Long-billed Plover - Arunachal Pradesh - India (34352332021).jpg|thumb|375x375px|A long-billed plover with its adult plumage]]
The long-billed plover is a medium-sized [[Wader|wading bird]] about {{Convert|19–20|cm|in|abbr=off}}, and weighing around {{Convert|41–70|g|oz|abbr=out}}.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Wk_CAwAAQBAJ&q=long-billed+plover&pg=PP1|title=Birds of East Asia: China, Taiwan, Korea, Japan, and Russia|last=Brazil|first=Mark|date=2009-01-01|publisher=A&C Black|isbn=978-0-691-13926-5|language=en}}</ref> The different body parts of males and females are similar in size. On average, the wing length is {{Convert|14–15|cm|in|abbr=out}}, its tail length {{Convert|7–8|cm|in|abbr=out}}, and the bill is about {{Convert|2|cm|in|abbr=out}} long.<ref name=":2" /> Both males and females have a grayish brown back and a white belly and throat.<ref name=":2" /> The forehead is white with a black band that runs from one eye to the other. The white patch of the forehead extends to the base of the bill. There is a black breast band below the throat.<ref name=":1" /> The eyes have a yellow ring around them, and a white stripe above.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /> Even though the male and female have a similar appearance, their breeding plumage (summer plumage) has a few differences that can help distinguish between the two sexes. The black band on the forehead and on the breast of the male is wider and more distinct than that of the female.<ref name=":2" /> The feathers around the eyes of an adult breeding male are much darker than those of a female. Furthermore, the male's white eyestripe extends more distinctly behind the eye compared to that of the female.<ref name=":1" /> Juveniles look quite similar to adult individuals, however they lack the dark forehead band of adult birds. The breast band of juveniles is narrower and less distinct than that of adult males.<ref name=":1" />

=== Confusion with other similar species ===
[[File:Charadrius dubius Scopoli.jpg|thumb|272x272px|Adult male little-ringed plover with breeding plumage]]
The long-billed plover is often confused with other species of plover such as the [[Little ringed plover|little-ringed plover]], because of range overlap and similarity in appearance.{{cn|date=February 2025}} However, the two species can still be distinguished from each other. The long-billed plover is larger than the little-ringed plover.{{cn|date=February 2025}} As the name suggests, the long-billed plover has a longer and thicker bill than the little-ringed plover. Adult breeding male individuals of little-ringed plover have a solid black bar above the base of the bill, in addition to the black forehead band.<ref name=":1" /> The feathers around the eyes of little-ringed plover are black unlike the brown cheek feathers of long-billed plover. Furthermore, the yellow eye-ring of the little-ringed plover is much more distinct than that of long-billed plover.<ref name=":1" /> The breast band of the little-ringed plover is usually wider at the center than on the sides, whereas the breast band of long-billed plover has more or less uniform thickness.<ref name=":1" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
The long-billed plover is widely distributed throughout [[South Asia|South]] and [[East Asia]]. It is quite uncommon throughout its range and occurs in small numbers.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last1=Kolomiyestev|first1=Nikolay|last2=Poddubnaya|first2=Nadezhda|date=2014|title=Breeding biology of the Long-billed Plover Charadrius placidus in the northern part of its range in the Russian Far East|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/272975522|journal=Wader Study Group Bulletin|volume=121|issue=3|pages=181–185|access-date=2019-10-13|via=ResearchGate}}</ref> Breeding ground generally includes western, northern, and central parts of [[China]], [[Japan]], [[North Korea]], and [[South Korea]].<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Lee|first1=Mu-Yeong|last2=Jeon|first2=Hey Sook|last3=Lee|first3=Sang-Hwa|last4=An|first4=Junghwa|date=2017-01-01|title=The mitochondrial genome of the long-billed plover, Charadrius placidus (Charadriiformes: Charadriidae)|journal=Mitochondrial DNA Part B|volume=2|issue=1|pages=122–123|doi=10.1080/23802359.2017.1292473|pmid=33473738|pmc=7800784|doi-access=free}}</ref> Many instances of breeding activity have been reported in the [[Bureya River|Bureya river]] and other parts of far east [[Russia]], [[Honshu|Honshu island]] in [[Japan]], southeastern [[China]], and in the western [[Arunachal Pradesh]] in [[India]] near the [[Himalayas]].<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last1=Eaton|first1=James A.|last2=Rheindt|first2=Frank E.|date=2009|title=Long-billed Plover Charadrius placidus nesting in its Himalayan wintering range: first breeding record for the Indian Subcontinent|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/274388026|journal=Forktail|volume=25|pages=152–153|access-date=2019-10-13|via=ResearchGate}}</ref> This species has also been spotted during its breeding season in [[Singapore]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Konrad|first=Volker|date=2005|title=First record of Long-billed Plover Charadrius placidus in Singapore|url=https://static1.squarespace.com/static/5c1a9e03f407b482a158da87/t/5c1ffaf00e2e7264c5341e70/1545599728935/Konrad-Plover.pdf|journal=Forktail|volume=21|pages=181–182|access-date=2019-10-13}}</ref> The long-billed plover travels south of its breeding range to spend the winter.<ref name=":6" /> It generally winters throughout eastern [[Nepal]], [[northeast India]] to Indochina, [[Northern and southern China|southern China]], [[South Korea]], [[Honshu]], [[Shikoku]], and [[Kyushu|Kiyushu]] islands of [[Japan]], [[Taiwan]], [[Myanmar]], [[Malaysia]], and [[Thailand]].<ref name=":6" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":5" /><ref name=":1" /> Populations on the three main islands of [[Japan]] do not generally migrate south, and are year-round residents. Only the population in [[Hokkaido]] travel south to winter in warmer climates.<ref name=":2" />
[[File:Long-billed Plover - Arunachal Pradesh - India FJ0A8807 (34155916121).jpg|thumb|339x339px|Long-billed plover in its ideal habitat]]
Long-billed plovers prefer to inhabit the shores of rivers, streams, and lakes with small, round pebbles and rocks.<ref name=":6" /> They tend to nest on small temporary shingle islands or pebble spits that form between the different branches of meandering mountain rivers.<ref name=":4" /> These islands cannot be prone to frequent flooding, and need to be at least {{Convert|600|m2|ft2|abbr=out}} in area. The islands also need to have large patches of stones and pebbles that are {{Convert|30–60|mm|in|abbr=out}} in size.<ref name=":4" /> Their habitats are often surrounded by [[shrub]]s, [[willow]]s, and young forests.<ref name=":4" /> Long-billed plovers can also be found in freshwater [[wetland]] habitats and rice fields in the winter.<ref name=":1" /> These birds tend to avoid sandy beaches, [[mudflat]]s, and areas with large boulders.<ref name=":6" />

== Behavior ==

=== Vocalization ===
The long-billed plover has a wide variety of calls for different situations. They tend to get highly vocal during the breeding season. They can be seen flapping their wings rapidly, flying over their territories, and emitting a "fi-fi-fi-fi-fi" call.<ref name=":2" /> Males sometimes make a quiet "gee gee" call when they expand their chest to intimidate other rival males.<ref name=":2" /> When they are vigilant, they make a single rising "pyuoo-" call. When they are sheltering themselves from a predator or any threat, they emit a call like, "pip-pip-pip".<ref name=":2" />

===Food and feeding===
Very little information is available on the feeding habit of the long-billed plover. Its diet is thought to primarily include aquatic insects and other [[invertebrate]]s.<ref name=":2" /> These birds have been observed to prey on [[midge]]s, [[Dragonfly|dragonflies]], [[larva]]e of [[beetle]]s, [[Fly|flies]], and [[earthworm]]s.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite journal|url=http://www.hbw.com/species/long-billed-plover-charadrius-placidus|title=Long-billed Plover (Charadrius placidus)|website=www.hbw.com|doi=10.2173/bow.lobplo1.01.1 |language=en|access-date=2019-10-14}}</ref> Long-billed plovers forage along the shoreline and when they spot a prey, they move towards it quickly and capture it with their long bill. They do not seem to consume any plant material.<ref name=":2" />

Similar species in the genus ''[[Charadrius]]'' such as the [[Common ringed plover]] and the [[Kentish plover]] have been observed to prey on [[Mysida|mysid]] shrimps.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Moran|first1=SH.|last2=Fishelson|first2=L.|date=1971-04-01|title=Predation of a sand-dwelling mysid crustacean Gastrosaccus sanctus by plover birds (Charadriidae)|journal=Marine Biology|language=en|volume=9|issue=1|pages=63–64|doi=10.1007/BF00348818|bibcode=1971MarBi...9...63M |s2cid=85217070|issn=1432-1793}}</ref> The diet of long-billed plovers may also consist of similar crustaceans.

=== Breeding ===
Long-billed plovers exhibit a [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous breeding system]] in which a male and a female form a pair and establish a territory that they maintain.<ref name=":2" /> Females, however, can abandon their nests sometimes to breed with other males. Males generally do not leave their territories.<ref name=":2" /> Both males and females take part in territorial disputes. They fly around their territory and let out loud calls. They puff their chest, and often jump, and peck to ward off any unwelcoming intruder.<ref name=":2" /> Males begin their breeding activity towards the end of February or in early March. The male displaces gravel with its feet and makes a shallow pit or depression, about {{Convert|4|cm|in|abbr=out}} deep and {{Convert|13|cm|in|abbr=out}} in diameter, by pressing its chest against the ground.<ref name=":2" /> The male attracts a female by calling her. When a female comes to the nest, the male spreads his tail for courtship display. The female passes under the tail of the male and enters the nest.<ref name=":2" /> The male makes many of these pits on the ground and guides the female from one pit to another, and the female eventually lays eggs in one of nests.<ref name=":2" /> The female lays one egg every 48 hours until she has a clutch of four eggs.<ref name=":4" /> The diameter and length of an egg is typically around {{Convert|3.5 and 2.6|cm|in|abbr=out}} respectively. The eggs generally weigh around {{Convert|10–13|g|oz|abbr=out}}.<ref name=":4" /> The eggs are pear-shaped, and they are grayish brown or bluish gray in color with tiny brown spots.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":2" /> Both male and female take part in nest maintenance. They line their nest with small pebbles, twigs, small pieces of bark, and other dry plant parts.<ref name=":4" /> Both male and female also carry out incubation of eggs. Eggs normally hatch 29 days after the start of incubation. Incubation period can vary because of human disturbance.<ref name=":2" /> Long-billed plovers show a decent amount of parental care. If a predator approaches a nest, the parents try to intimidate the enemy and chase it away by making loud distress calls.<ref name=":2" /> Furthermore, on very hot summer days, the parents protect the eggs from overheating by standing over them and shading them from the sun.<ref name=":4" /> Long-billed plovers give birth to [[Precociality|precocial]] offspring, meaning that the hatchlings can move around and feed by themselves shortly after hatching. The parents constantly monitor the hatchlings and are always on the lookout for any signs of danger.<ref name=":2" /> The hatchlings gain body mass at the rate of {{Convert|1|g|oz|abbr=out}} per day, and they become ready to fly when they are 40 days old.<ref name=":4" /> [[Fledge|Fledglings]] remain together with their parents for a short time before they leave the nest and migrate to their wintering grounds.<ref name=":4" />

Long-billed plovers tend to return to the same breeding for several consecutive years. This indicates that the same pairs occupy the same nesting sites every year.<ref name=":4" /> The pairs tend to be attracted to the same breeding sites year after year. They begin to establish territories when the area still remains covered with snow, suggesting that they must have previous knowledge of the suitability of the site.<ref name=":4" />

== Conservation status ==
The long-billed plover is listed as a species of least concern on the IUCN Red List because of its wide distribution and large home range. It has a distribution range that exceeds {{Convert|20,000|km2|mi2|abbr=out}}.<ref name=":7">{{Cite web|url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/Long-billed-Plover|title=Long-billed Plover Charadrius placidus|website=BirdLife|access-date=2019-10-14}}</ref> The global population seems to be decreasing, but the rate of decline is not drastic enough to make this species vulnerable. Population decline has to be more than 30% over ten years or three generations for a species to be deemed vulnerable.<ref name=":7" /> Even though long-billed plovers are not vulnerable or threatened now, their population might decline rapidly in the future due to the loss of suitable breeding grounds.<ref name=":8">{{Cite journal|last1=Kim|first1=In-Kyu|last2=Lee|first2=Hansoo|last3=Paek|first3=Woon-Kee|last4=Yoo|first4=Seung-Hwa|last5=Lee|first5=Joon-Woo|date=2009-09-01|title=Distribution and Conservation of Long-billed Plovers Charadrius placidus in Korea|journal=Journal of Korean Nature|volume=2|issue=2|pages=115–117|doi=10.1016/S1976-8648(14)60047-5|issn=1976-8648|doi-access=free}}</ref> Conservation efforts should include protection and restoration of breeding habitats. Habitat restoration projects can provide new breeding habitats through the artificial construction of gravel banks and pebble islands.<ref name=":8" />

zh.extract:
长嘴剑鸻（学名：Charadrius placidus），台湾又称为长嘴鸻、剑鸻，为鸻科鸻属的鸟类。 它分佈在孟加拉國、不丹、汶萊、柬埔寨、中國、香港、印度、印尼、日本、寮國、馬來西亞、蒙古、緬甸、尼泊爾、北韓、俄羅斯、南韓、斯里蘭卡、台灣、泰國和越南。 在中国大陆，分布于华南、华北等地。该物种的模式产地在尼泊尔。
劍鴴是候鳥，因此它在不同區域繁殖和過冬。 這種鳥類經常出現在河流、溪流的岸邊，濕地和稻田中。 它主要在岸邊覓食水生昆蟲、昆蟲幼蟲和其他無脊椎動物。 由於雄性和雌性個體擁有相似的羽毛，所以很難區分牠們的性別。 繁殖季節從二月底或三月初開始，至七月結束。 一對雄性和雌性會形成單配偶制關係，並在整個繁殖季節維護牠們的領域。 2016年的一項全球種群調查將劍鴴評估為國際自然保護聯盟瀕危物種紅色名錄上的無危物種。


描述


體型與羽毛
  
劍鴴是一種中型涉禽，體長約19—20厘米（7.5—7.9英寸），體重約41—70克（1.4—2.5 oz）。 雄性和雌性個體的不同身體部位大小相似。其翅長平均約為14—15厘米（5.5—5.9英寸），尾長約7—8厘米（2.8—3.1英寸），嘴長約2厘米（0.79英寸）。 雄性和雌性都有灰棕色的背部，腹部和喉部為白色。 前額為白色，有一條從一隻眼睛延伸到另一隻眼睛的黑色帶。額前的白色延伸到嘴基部。喉部下方有一條黑色胸帶。 眼睛周圍有一圈黃色環，且上方有一條白色條紋。 儘管雄性和雌性外觀相似，但牠們的繁殖羽（夏羽）有一些差異，可以幫助區分性別。雄性前額和胸部的黑色帶比雌性更寬且更明顯。 成年繁殖期雄性眼周的羽毛比雌性更暗。此外，雄性的白色眼帶比雌性更明顯地延伸至眼後。 幼鳥與成鳥相似，但缺少成鳥額前的黑色帶。幼鳥的胸帶比成年雄性更窄且不如其明顯。


與其他類似物種的混淆
  
由於分佈區重疊且外觀相似，劍鴴常常與其他鴴科物種如小環頸鴴混淆。 然而，這兩個物種仍然可以區分開來。劍鴴比小環頸鴴更大。 顧名思義，劍鴴的嘴比小環頸鴴的嘴更長且更粗。成年的小環頸鴴雄鳥在嘴基部上方除了有黑色額前帶外，還有一條明顯的黑色橫帶。 小環頸鴴眼周的羽毛為黑色，與劍鴴的棕色臉頰羽毛不同。此外，小環頸鴴的黃色眼圈比劍鴴的眼圈更為明顯。 小環頸鴴的胸帶在中央通常較寬，而劍鴴的胸帶則厚度較為均勻。


分類
劍鴴是鴴科屬於鴴屬（Charadrius）的多種鴴鳥之一，該科包括鴴、麥雞和小嘴鴴。 鴴科是鴴形目下的17個科之一，該目包含多種類的岸鳥，如鷗、燕鷗、海雀、海鸚、鷸、麥雞、鴴等。 劍鴴最早由J. E. Gray和G. R. Gray於1863年描述，至今未有報導任何亞種。


分佈與棲地
劍鴴廣泛分佈於南亞和東亞。在其分佈範圍內相當罕見，且數量稀少。 其繁殖地主要包括中國的西部、北部和中部地區、日本、北韓和南韓。 在俄羅斯遠東地區的布列亞河及其他地區、日本的本州島、中國東南部及印度西部的阿魯納恰爾邦靠近喜馬拉雅山的地區，曾報導過多次繁殖活動。 此物種在繁殖季節也曾在新加坡被觀察到。 劍鴴會向其繁殖範圍的南部遷徙過冬。 它通常在東尼泊爾、印度東北部至中南半島、中國南部、南韓、本州島、四國島、九州島（日本）、台灣、緬甸、馬來西亞和泰國過冬。 日本三個主要島嶼上的種群通常不遷徙南下，而是全年定居在原地。只有北海道的種群會南下到較溫暖的氣候過冬。  

  
劍鴴偏好棲息在有小圓卵石和岩石的河流、溪流和湖泊岸邊。 它們傾向於在蜿蜒山河的不同支流之間形成的小型臨時鵝卵石島或卵石沙洲上築巢。 這些島嶼不能經常受到洪水侵襲，面積需要至少600平方米（6,500 sq ft）。這些島嶼還需要有大面積直徑約為30—60毫米（1.2—2.4英寸）的石頭和卵石。 它們的棲地通常被灌木、柳樹和幼樹環繞。 劍鴴在冬季也可以在淡水濕地棲地和稻田中找到。 這些鳥類通常會避開沙灘、泥灘和有大岩石的區域。


行為


聲音
劍鴴有多種針對不同情境的叫聲。它們在繁殖季節時變得非常吵鬧。可以看到它們快速拍打翅膀，飛越自己的領地，並發出「fi-fi-fi-fi-fi」的叫聲。 雄鳥有時會在擴展胸部以威嚇其他雄性對手時，發出低沉的「gee gee」叫聲。 當牠們保持警戒時，會發出單一上升的「pyuoo-」叫聲。當牠們避開捕食者或任何威脅時，會發出像「pip-pip-pip」的叫聲。


覓食
關於劍鴴的飲食習性信息非常有限。據推測，其飲食主要包括水生昆蟲和其他無脊椎動物。 已觀察到這些鳥類捕食搖蚊、蜻蜓、甲蟲幼蟲、蒼蠅和蚯蚓。 劍鴴沿著岸邊覓食，當牠們發現獵物時，會迅速移向獵物並用長嘴捕捉。牠們似乎不攝取任何植物材料。  
與鴴屬（Charadrius）中的相似物種，如環頸鴴和東方環頸鴴，已被觀察到捕食糠蝦。 劍鴴的飲食可能也包括類似的甲殼類動物。


繁殖
劍鴴展現單配偶制的繁殖系統，雄鳥和雌鳥形成配偶關係並建立並維持領地。 然而，雌鳥有時會離開牠們的巢穴，與其他雄鳥交配。雄鳥通常不會離開牠們的領地。 雄鳥和雌鳥都會參與領地爭奪戰。牠們在領地內飛翔，並發出響亮的叫聲。牠們鼓起胸部，經常跳起並啄擊來驅趕不受歡迎的入侵者。 雄鳥於二月底或三月初開始其繁殖活動。雄鳥用腳移動礫石，並壓低胸部在地面上製作一個淺坑或凹陷，約4厘米（1.6英寸）深、13厘米（5.1英寸）直徑。 雄鳥通過叫聲吸引雌鳥。當雌鳥來到巢穴時，雄鳥展開尾羽進行求偶展示。雌鳥從雄鳥的尾羽下方通過並進入巢穴。 雄鳥在地面上製作許多這樣的凹坑，並引導雌鳥從一個坑移到另一個坑，最終雌鳥會在其中一個巢中產卵。 雌鳥每隔48小時產下一顆蛋，直到牠有四顆蛋。 一顆蛋的直徑和長度通常分別約為3.5和2.6厘米（1.4、1.0英寸）。蛋的重量一般約為10—13克（0.35—0.46 oz）。 蛋呈梨形，顏色為灰褐色或藍灰色，帶有細小的棕色斑點。 雄鳥和雌鳥共同參與巢穴的維護。牠們用小卵石、樹枝、小片樹皮和其他乾燥植物部位襯墊巢穴。 雄鳥和雌鳥也共同孵卵。從開始孵卵到蛋孵化通常需要29天。孵化期可能會因人類干擾而有所變化。 劍鴴展現出相當程度的父母照護。如果捕食者接近巢穴，親鳥會試圖威嚇並通過發出響亮的驚擾叫聲將其趕走。 此外，在非常炎熱的夏日，親鳥會通過站在蛋上方，為蛋遮擋陽光，以防過熱。 劍鴴產下的幼鳥屬於早熟性，意味著幼鳥在孵化後不久就能四處活動並自行覓食。親鳥不斷監視幼鳥，隨時注意任何危險的跡象。 幼鳥的體重增長速度為每天1克（0.035 oz），牠們在40天大時準備好飛行。 雛鳥羽翼長成後並遷徙到牠們的冬季棲地之前，會與親鳥短暫地待在一起。  
劍鴴傾向於連續數年返回相同的繁殖地。這表明同一對配偶每年都佔據相同的巢址。 配偶每年都傾向於被吸引到相同的繁殖地。牠們在地區仍被積雪覆蓋時就開始建立領地，這表明牠們必須在先前已了解該地點的適宜性。


保育狀況
由於劍鴴分佈廣泛且家域範圍大，它被IUCN紅色名錄列為無危物種。其分佈範圍超過20,000平方公里（7,700 sq mi）。 全球種群似乎正在減少，但下降的速度不足以使該物種變得易危。物種被視為易危的前提是其種群下降幅度在十年或三代內超過30%。 雖然目前劍鴴並不易危或受威脅，但由於適宜繁殖地的喪失，牠們的種群在未來可能會迅速下降。 保育措施應包括保護和恢復繁殖棲地。通過人工建造礫石河岸和卵石島嶼的棲地恢復項目可以提供新的繁殖棲地。


参考文献

 长嘴鸻 Charadrius placidus Gray & Gray, 1863. 台湾物种名录. 台湾: 中央研究院生物多样性研究中心.   [2013-01-24] （中文（台湾））.
